stem: A school principal wants to find out how many hours per week students at her school spend on homework. She surveys 30 students who are members of the school's academic honors club and finds that, on average, these students spend 12 hours per week on homework. The principal concludes that the typical student at her school spends 12 hours per week on homework. Which of the following best explains why the principal's conclusion is NOT valid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sample size of 30 students is too small to draw any conclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The survey only included honors club members, who likely spend more time on homework than typical students, so the sample does not represent all students at the school.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The principal should have asked students to report daily homework time instead of weekly homework time.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The average of 12 hours per week is too high to be believable for most students.

explanation: The principal's conclusion is not valid because the sample was not randomly selected from all students at the school. The 30 students surveyed are all members of the honors club, a group that is likely to do significantly more homework than a typical student. This is a biased convenience sample — it selects only a specific subgroup rather than representing the full student population. Because honors club members are not representative of all students, any average calculated from this group cannot be generalized to the entire school. A valid conclusion about all students at the school would require a randomly selected sample from the full student body.

distractorRationale.A: While sample size matters, the primary flaw here is not the size but the biased selection method. Thirty students could be sufficient for a valid estimate if they were randomly chosen from all students.
distractorRationale.B: Correct. The sample is drawn exclusively from honors club members, a group with systematically different homework habits than the general student population. This selection bias makes the conclusion invalid.
distractorRationale.C: The unit of time (weekly vs. daily) is irrelevant to the validity of the conclusion. Converting between weekly and daily would not fix the sampling problem.
distractorRationale.D: Whether 12 hours seems high or low is an opinion and not a statistical reason for the conclusion being invalid. The flaw lies in how the sample was chosen, not in the numerical result.
